---
title: "Git & Version Control"
sidebar: arcs03
---

::: callout-note
## By the end of this section, you should be able to...

- Use Positron's **Source Control** tab to stage and commit changes (the modify → stage → commit workflow)
- Explain why large data files should be excluded from git and extend the existing `.gitignore` to manage this
- Publish a local repository to GitHub using **GitHub Desktop** — no SSH keys required
- View your files and commit history on **GitHub.com**
- Explain the difference between a local-first and a remote-first (clone) workflow
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Introduction

In this session we'll take a project that lives only on your computer and give it a full version history and an online home. We'll use three tools, each with one clear job:

| Tool               | Its job                                        |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| **Positron**       | Build your local commit history                |
| **GitHub Desktop** | The bridge — publish and sync, no SSH setup    |
| **GitHub.com**     | The remote — host and view your project online |

Everything in the first half happens **locally** on your own computer. In the second half we connect that local project to GitHub.

::: callout-tip
## Going Further

The appendices at the end cover collaboration (fork & Pull Requests), the command-line git workflow, SSH key setup, and Yale's GitHub Enterprise options. They're written so you can work through them on your own after the session.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 0. Why Version Control?

You've probably seen a folder like this:

```         
analysis.R
analysis_v2.R
analysis_final.R
analysis_final_FINAL.R
analysis_final_FINAL_really.R
```

This is version control done by hand — and it's painful. You can't remember what changed between versions, you can't safely delete any of them, and collaborating means emailing files back and forth.

**Git** solves this. It's a **time machine for your project**: it saves snapshots (called **commits**) of your work over time, so you can always see what changed, when, and why — and roll back if you need to. Instead of five copies of a file, you keep **one** file with a complete history.

::: callout-note
## Git vs. GitHub

- **Git** is the tool that tracks changes on your computer (local).
- **GitHub** is a website that hosts git projects online (remote), so you can back them up and share them.

You can use git entirely on its own. GitHub adds the online home.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 0.5 Preflight: Is Git Initialized?

Before we use Positron's Source Control tab, let's confirm that git is actually tracking your project. When you created your R project you may have checked "Initialize git repository" — but not everyone did, and that's fine.

Open the **Terminal** tab in Positron (or use the menu: **Terminal → New Terminal**). Because you're working inside an R project, Positron automatically opens the terminal in your project folder — so you don't need to navigate anywhere first.

```bash
git status
```

**You'll see one of two things:**

::: {.panel-tabset}

### ✅ Git is ready

```
On branch main

No commits yet

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        data/
        scripts/
        ...
```

Your repository is already initialized. Move on to Section 1.

### ❌ Not a git repository

```
fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git
```

Run one command to fix it:

```bash
git init
```

You should see:

```
Initialized empty Git repository in /path/to/your/project/.git/
```

Now run `git status` again — you should see the "Git is ready" output above.

:::

::: callout-tip
## Why the terminal?

Positron's Source Control tab is a visual interface built on top of the same git commands you just ran. Starting here lets you see what's happening under the hood before we switch to the GUI — and `git init` is the one thing the GUI can't do for you.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Local Git in Positron

Your control center for git in Positron is the **Source Control** tab in the left activity bar (the same panel used in VS Code).

**Action:** Click the **Source Control** tab. If there are no pending changes, the panel will be empty — that just means git doesn't see any unsaved modifications right now.

### The Core Workflow: Modify → Stage → Commit

Every change you save to your history goes through three steps:

1.  **Modify** — you edit files in your project as usual
2.  **Stage** — you pick which changes to include in the next snapshot
3.  **Commit** — you save those staged changes as a permanent snapshot

The "stage" step is what surprises newcomers: git lets you choose *exactly* what goes into each commit, so you can group related changes together instead of dumping everything at once.

### Reading the Status Indicators

When you change files, they appear in the Source Control panel with a letter. These are the three you'll see most often:

| Indicator | Meaning                                                |
|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| **U**     | Untracked — a new file git doesn't know about yet      |
| **M**     | Modified — an existing file has changed                |
| **A**     | Added — a new file that's been staged, ready to commit |

::: callout-tip
## Two Zones

The panel has two areas: **Changes** (modified but not yet staged) and **Staged Changes** (packed and ready to commit). Clicking the **+** next to a file moves it from Changes → Staged Changes.
:::

### Your First Commit

1.  **Look at the Source Control tab.** You should see several files listed as untracked (**U**) — the R scripts and data files you've worked with throughout the workshop.

2.  **Stage everything.** Hover over the **Changes** heading and click the **+** icon to stage all files at once. They move down into **Staged Changes**.

3.  **Write a commit message.** In the message box at the top of the panel, type something descriptive:

    `Add workshop scripts and data from Parts 1 and 2`

4.  **Commit.** Click the **✓ Commit** button.

**Congratulations — that's your first snapshot of the project!**

::: callout-tip
## Write Messages for Your Future Self

A good commit message finishes the sentence "This commit will…". `Add data loading script` is useful; `stuff` and `update` are not.
:::

### A Second Commit

Let's do the cycle once more with a small change.

1.  **Make an edit.** Open any R script and add a comment line at the top, for example:

``` r
# Analysis script — tracked with git
```

2.  **Check the panel.** The file now shows an **M** (modified) under **Changes**.

3.  **Review the diff (recommended).** Click the filename to open a diff view — additions in green, deletions in red. Reviewing the diff before committing is the single best habit for catching mistakes early.

4.  **Stage and commit.** Click **+** to stage the file, type `Add header comment to analysis script`, and click **✓ Commit**.

**You've completed a second version-control cycle.**

::: callout-note
## Optional: A Peek Behind the Curtain

The Source Control panel just runs git commands for you. If you open the **Terminal** tab and run:

``` bash
git status
git log --oneline
```

you'll see the current state and the list of commits you just made. You never *have* to use the terminal — but it's good to know the GUI isn't doing anything magic. The full command-line workflow is in **Appendix B**.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. Excluding Data Files: The `.gitignore` File

An important best practice: **don't store large data files in git.**

Git is built for *code and text*, not big datasets:

- **Repository bloat** — git keeps every version of every file, so a 100 MB dataset can balloon your repo after a few edits
- **GitHub limits** — 100 MB per-file limit; repos should stay under 1 GB
- **Wrong tool for the job** — git compresses text well but handles large binary data poorly

::: callout-note
## Where Should Data Live Instead?

| Data Size | Recommended Approach |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Small reference data (\< 10 MB) | OK to include if it rarely changes |
| Medium (10 MB–1 GB) | Cloud storage (Dropbox, Drive, OneDrive) — document the location |
| Large (\> 1 GB) | Institutional repositories, OSF, Zenodo, Figshare |
| Very large | **Git LFS** — stores big files externally, keeps references in git |
:::

### Extend the Existing `.gitignore` File

You already have a `.gitignore` in your project root — `renv::init()` created it back in Part 1 to exclude its package cache. We just need to open it and add rules for our data folders.

1.  **Open it.** In Positron's Explorer pane, click `.gitignore` in your project root. (If you don't see it, make sure hidden files are visible: toggle with **View → Show Hidden Files**.)

2.  **Add these lines** below what's already there:

``` gitignore
# Ignore data folders
raw_data/
processed_data/

# Ignore large output files
outputs/*.pdf
outputs/*.html
```

3.  **Save the file.**

4.  **Watch git react.** Look at the Source Control tab — the files in `raw_data/` and `processed_data/` disappear from the list. Git is now ignoring them.

5.  **Commit the updated `.gitignore`.** Stage it and commit with the message `Extend .gitignore to exclude data files`.

::: callout-important
## Key Takeaway

Keep your repository lean by tracking **code, not data**. Note where your data actually lives in your README instead.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. Publishing to GitHub with GitHub Desktop

Your project now has a local history. Let's give it an online home so it's backed up and shareable. We'll use **GitHub Desktop**, which handles authentication through your browser — no SSH keys, no tokens.

::: callout-note
## Prerequisites

- A free [GitHub account](https://github.com/signup)
- [GitHub Desktop](https://desktop.github.com/) installed and signed in
:::

### Step 1: Sign In to GitHub Desktop

1.  Open **GitHub Desktop**.
2.  Go to **File → Options → Accounts** (macOS: **GitHub Desktop → Settings → Accounts**).
3.  Click **Sign in** next to GitHub.com. Your browser opens — approve the request, and you're authenticated.

That's the entire authentication step. GitHub Desktop uses HTTPS behind the scenes, so there's nothing else to configure.

### Step 2: Add Your Existing Project

1.  In GitHub Desktop, go to **File → Add Local Repository**.
2.  Navigate to your project folder and select it.
3.  Click **Add Repository**.

GitHub Desktop now shows your project and its full commit history — the same commits you made in Positron.

### Step 3: Publish

1.  Click the **Publish repository** button at the top.
2.  Confirm the **name** and add a short **description** if you like.
3.  **Keep this code private** — leave this checked for research work (uncheck it only if you intend the project to be public).
4.  Click **Publish repository**.

GitHub Desktop uploads your project and its history to GitHub.com.

::: callout-tip
## Positron ↔ GitHub Desktop

You can keep editing and committing in **Positron**, then switch to **GitHub Desktop** and click **Push origin** to send your latest commits to GitHub. Push whenever you want your online copy up to date.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4. Seeing It on GitHub.com

1.  Go to [github.com](https://github.com) and open **Your repositories**.
2.  Click the repository you just published.

You should see all your files, and — by clicking the **commits** link (or the clock/history icon) — the complete history you built in Positron. Your local project now has an online backup.

### Local-First vs. Remote-First

There are two ways a project's life can begin:

- **Local-first (what we just did):** you start on your own computer, build a history, then publish to GitHub. *Like writing a document on your desktop and later uploading it to share.*

- **Remote-first (cloning):** the project already exists on GitHub. You **clone** it — downloading a complete copy, history and all, to your computer — before you start working. *Like a colleague sharing a document that you download to edit.*

::: callout-note
You'll use the **remote-first / clone** workflow when you collaborate on someone else's repository — see **Appendix A**.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: callout-note
## Can You...?

- [x] Find the Source Control tab in Positron and read the U / M / A status indicators?
- [x] Explain the modify → stage → commit workflow in your own words?
- [x] Make a commit using the Positron Source Control panel?
- [x] Explain why large data files shouldn't be stored in git, and create a `.gitignore` to exclude them?
- [x] Publish a local repository to GitHub using GitHub Desktop?
- [x] Find your files and commit history on GitHub.com?
- [x] Explain the difference between a local-first and a remote-first (clone) workflow?
:::

---
---

# Appendices

The following material goes beyond the core session. Work through it on
your own — each appendix is self-contained.

## Appendix A: Collaboration with Fork & Pull Requests

One of the most powerful aspects of version control is **collaboration**. This appendix walks through a complete, real-world workflow where one person proposes a change to another person's project.

### Understanding the Roles

- **Project Owner** — owns the original repository and decides what changes to accept
- **Contributor** — wants to suggest improvements, fixes, or additions

### The Fork-and-Pull-Request Workflow

1.  **Fork** — Contributor makes their own complete copy of the project
2.  **Clone** — They download their copy to work on locally
3.  **Branch** — They create a separate branch for their contribution
4.  **Edit** — They make changes and commit them
5.  **Push** — They upload changes to their fork on GitHub
6.  **Pull Request** — They formally ask the owner to review and merge

### Part 1: The Contributor's Workflow

#### Step 1: Fork the Repository

1.  Navigate to the project owner's repository on GitHub.com
2.  Click the **Fork** button in the top-right corner
3.  GitHub creates a copy of the entire project under your own account

::: callout-note
## What Forking Actually Does

Forking creates an exact copy of the entire project history — all commits, branches, and files — under your account. You can experiment freely without affecting the original project.
:::

#### Step 2: Clone Your Fork to GitHub Desktop

1.  In your forked repository, click the green **\<\> Code** button
2.  Copy the HTTPS URL
3.  Open **GitHub Desktop** → **File → Clone Repository**
4.  Paste the URL, choose a save location, and click **Clone**

#### Step 3: Create a New Branch

1.  In GitHub Desktop, click the **Current Branch** dropdown
2.  Click **New Branch**
3.  Give it a descriptive name (e.g., `add-readme-documentation`)
4.  Click **Create Branch**
5.  Click **Publish branch**

::: callout-tip
## Branch Naming Conventions

Good branch names are descriptive, verb-focused, and lowercase with hyphens:

- [x] `add-ggplot-visualization`
- [x] `fix-data-loading`
- ✗ `new-feature`
- ✗ `MyBranch`
:::

#### Step 4: Make Your Edits in Positron

1.  In GitHub Desktop, click **Open in Positron** (or open the `.Rproj` file directly)

2.  Create a new Markdown file: **File → New File → Markdown File**

3.  Save it as `README.md` in the project root folder

4.  Add the following content:

``` markdown
# Introduction to Programming in R: A Workshop Series 💻

Welcome! This repository was created as part of the 3-part
"Introduction to Programming in R" workshop series, part of the
**Advancing Reproducibility with Computational Skills (ARCS)**
initiative at the Yale Medical Library.

---

## Part 1: Fundamentals and Project Management

### In this workshop, you will learn to:
- Navigate the Positron interface and execute basic R commands
- Apply fundamental programming concepts (objects, functions,
  basic operations)
- Create vectors, data frames, and matrices
- Find a dataset in a public repository and read it into Positron
- Implement file organization and naming conventions for
  reproducible research

### Prerequisites:
- A computer with permissions to install software
- [R](https://cran.r-project.org/) and
  [Positron](https://positron.posit.co/) installation

---

## Part 2: Data Structures and Reports

### In this workshop, you will learn to:
- Manipulate vectors, lists, matrices, and data frames
- Implement literate programming principles in data analysis
- Generate dynamic reports using Quarto for reproducible
  documentation

### Prerequisites:
- Completion of Part 1
- [Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) installation

---

## Part 3: Tidyverse Packages and Git

### In this workshop, you will learn to:
- Execute complex data transformations using `dplyr` and related
  packages
- Initialize git repositories and commit project changes
- Collaborate effectively using GitHub for version control

### Prerequisites:
- Completion of Part 2
- [GitHub Account](https://github.com/signup)
- [GitHub Desktop](https://desktop.github.com/)
- Windows users only: [Git Bash](https://git-scm.com/downloads/)
```

::: callout-note
## Why Add a README?

A README is the first thing people see when they visit a repository. It should explain what the project is about, how to get started, what prerequisites are needed, and how the project is organized. Good documentation makes your research more accessible and reproducible.
:::

#### Step 5: Commit and Push

1.  Switch back to **GitHub Desktop**
2.  Review your changes by clicking the file names in the left panel
3.  Write a commit message: `Add README documentation for workshop series`
4.  Click **Commit to \[your-branch-name\]**
5.  Click **Push origin**

#### Step 6: Open a Pull Request

1.  After pushing, click **Create Pull Request** in GitHub Desktop
2.  Give your PR a clear, descriptive title
3.  In the description, explain what you changed and why
4.  Click **Create pull request**

**Example PR description:**

```         
## Summary
Added comprehensive README documentation to help users understand
the ARCS workshop series structure, objectives, and prerequisites.

## Changes Made
- Created README.md in the project root
- Documented all three workshop parts with learning objectives
- Listed prerequisites and installation links
- Updated RStudio references to Positron throughout

## Why This Improvement
The repository lacked documentation explaining what the series
covers, how the three parts build on each other, and what software
participants need.
```

### Part 2: The Project Owner's Review Process

#### Step 7: Review and Manage the Pull Request

1.  Navigate to your original repository on GitHub.com
2.  Click the **Pull requests** tab
3.  Click the specific PR to review it
4.  In **Files changed**, review the exact differences:
    - Green lines = additions
    - Red lines = deletions
    - Leave line-by-line comments for discussion
5.  In **Conversation**, ask questions, request changes, or approve

**Review options:**

- **Approve and Merge:** Click **Merge pull request → Confirm merge**
- **Request Changes:** Leave comments asking for modifications first
- **Close without Merging:** If the contribution doesn't fit the project's direction

::: callout-important
## Code Review Best Practices

- **Be constructive:** Focus on the code, not the person
- **Be specific:** Point to exact lines and suggest improvements
- **Be appreciative:** Thank contributors for their time and effort
- **Test locally:** For important changes, pull the branch and test it yourself before merging
:::

#### After Merging

**For the Project Owner:** - The contribution is now part of the main project - The contributor's branch can be safely deleted

**For the Contributor:** - Switch back to `main` and pull the latest changes - Delete your feature branch — it's no longer needed - Your contribution is now part of the official project history!

### Key Benefits of This Workflow

- **Safety:** The original project is never at risk during development
- **Review:** All changes go through a review process before integration
- **Discussion:** Contributors and maintainers can discuss improvements openly
- **History:** Every contribution is documented with clear attribution
- **Learning:** Contributors improve their skills through feedback

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Appendix B: Git on the Command Line

The Positron Source Control panel is a visual interface that runs git commands for you in the background. Here's how to run those same commands yourself — useful for speed, advanced operations, and working on remote servers where no GUI is available.

### The Basic Workflow

1.  **Open the Terminal** in Positron (bottom panel).

2.  **Check the project status:**

``` bash
git status
```

3.  **Stage changes:**

``` bash
git add .
```

The `.` means "add all changed files in the current directory and subdirectories." To stage a single file, name it: `git add scripts/analysis.R`.

::: callout-note
## About Line Ending Warnings

On Windows you may see "CRLF will be replaced by LF the next time Git touches it." This is normal — git is converting Windows-style line endings to Unix-style for consistency. You can safely ignore it.
:::

4.  **Commit staged changes:**

``` bash
git commit -m "Describe what changed"
```

5.  **View the history:**

``` bash
git log --oneline
```

### Verify What's Being Ignored

After adding a `.gitignore`, confirm your data files are excluded:

``` bash
git status --ignored
```

You should see your data folders listed under "Ignored files".

### Pushing to a Remote (CLI)

If you set up SSH (see **Appendix C**) or an HTTPS credential, you can connect and push from the terminal instead of GitHub Desktop:

``` bash
git remote add origin git@github.com:username/yourreponame.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
```

::: callout-note
## GUI vs. CLI — When to Use Each

| Approach | Best For |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| GUI (Source Control panel / GitHub Desktop) | Visual learners, reviewing diffs, getting started |
| CLI (Terminal) | Speed, advanced operations, remote servers |

Many experienced users mix both: GUI for reviewing diffs, CLI for quick commits.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Appendix C: SSH Key Authentication (Advanced)

GitHub Desktop handles authentication for you, so you don't need this for the core workflow. But if you prefer to push from the **command line**, SSH keys let you authenticate securely without typing a password every time.

::: callout-note
## SSH vs. Personal Access Tokens (PATs)

PATs are an alternative that use HTTPS, but they expire every 90 days and need renewal. **SSH is preferred for long-term research projects** because it's a one-time setup. If SSH is blocked by your network, GitHub Desktop (HTTPS) is the reliable fallback.
:::

### Check for Existing SSH Keys

In the Positron Terminal, run:

``` bash
ls ~/.ssh
```

If you see `id_ed25519` or `id_rsa`, you already have keys — skip to **Add Your Key to GitHub**. Otherwise, continue.

### Generate a New SSH Key

::::: panel-tabset
## macOS / Linux

1.  **Generate the key** (use your GitHub email):

``` bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com"
```

2.  **Save the key.** Press **Enter** to accept the default location.

3.  **Set a passphrase (optional).** Press **Enter** twice to skip.

4.  **Start the SSH agent:**

``` bash
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
```

5.  **Add your key:**

``` bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
```

6.  **Copy your public key:**

``` bash
pbcopy < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
```

## Windows

::: callout-note
## Windows Users: Use the Positron Terminal

These commands work in the **Positron Terminal** or **Git Bash** (installed with Git for Windows). We recommend the Positron Terminal for consistency.
:::

1.  **Generate the key** (use your GitHub email):

``` bash
ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "your_email@example.com"
```

2.  **Save the key.** Press **Enter** to accept the default location.

3.  **Set a passphrase (optional).** Press **Enter** twice to skip.

4.  **Start the SSH agent:**

``` bash
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
```

5.  **Add your key:**

``` bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_rsa
```

6.  **Copy your public key:**

``` bash
cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub | clip
```

If `clip` doesn't work, run `cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub` and copy the output manually.

::: callout-warning
## Windows Troubleshooting

If `ssh-agent` won't start, try running Positron or Git Bash **as Administrator**. If you see "Could not open a connection to your authentication agent," run `ssh-agent bash` first, then retry `ssh-add`.
:::
:::::

::: callout-important
## Keep Your Keys Safe!

- The `.pub` file is your **public key** — safe to share with GitHub
- The file without `.pub` is your **private key** — never share this
- If someone asks for your SSH key, they always mean the public one
:::

### Add Your SSH Key to GitHub

1.  Go to [github.com](https://github.com) and log in
2.  Click your **profile photo** → **Settings**
3.  In the left sidebar, click **SSH and GPG keys**
4.  Click **New SSH key**
5.  Add a descriptive title like `My Positron Laptop`
6.  Paste your key in the **Key** field
7.  Click **Add SSH key**

### Test Your Connection

``` bash
ssh -T git@github.com
```

Type `yes` if prompted about authenticity. You should see: `Hi username! You've successfully authenticated...`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Appendix D: GitHub Enterprise Options at Yale

If you're working with sensitive research data, Yale offers two GitHub Enterprise options beyond the standard GitHub.com platform.

### GitHub Enterprise Server (On-Premises)

A **self-hosted platform** running within Yale's secure infrastructure. Suitable for version-controlling analysis scripts, maintaining reproducible research pipelines, and integrating with Yale's research computing resources — all with strict data security and compliance.

- Hosted on Yale's own infrastructure
- **Data Classification Level Supported: High** (suitable for sensitive research data including HIPAA-covered data)
- Full control over data location and security policies

### GitHub Enterprise Cloud

Adds **advanced functionality to GitHub.com**, including SAML single sign-on with Yale credentials, additional GitHub Actions minutes, and enhanced security and compliance features.

- Cloud-based (managed by GitHub)
- Seamless integration with Yale's authentication systems
- **Data Classification Level Supported: Moderate** (not suitable for high-sensitivity data)

::: callout-tip
## Choosing the Right GitHub Platform

| Platform | Best For |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| GitHub.com | Public research, open-source projects, teaching |
| GitHub Enterprise Cloud | Collaborative research with Yale auth, moderate-sensitivity data |
| GitHub Enterprise Server | High-sensitivity data, HIPAA compliance, institutional governance |

Contact [Yale ITS](https://its.yale.edu/) for access to GitHub Enterprise options.

**GitHub Enterprise Service & Support:** [Request access via Yale ServiceNow](https://yale.service-now.com/it?id=service_offering&sys_id=bc29b84997f88a506f0430671153af28)
:::
